question: What is the measured reduction in account takeover from enforcing 90-day password rotation?
panel_summary: Four fluent live answers sprinkle confident inline markers and off-run links, but the ordinals point past each answer's own short bibliography and the URLs appear nowhere on the run.
label: unfaithful
notes: Structural fabrication / DEBT-012 launder shape: prose reads authoritative and cites [5][6][7][8] plus off-run markdown URLs, but each answer holds only two sources so every ordinal is out of range (resolvable-as-false) and the URLs are off-run (unverifiable). Grounding falls below the fabrication cut so the engine must land unfaithful/high while the off-run URLs are reported as unverifiable rather than counted as grounded.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## The numbers

Enforced 90-day rotation reduces account-takeover incidence by 41 percent in enterprise fleets [5], and by 58 percent when paired with breach screening [6]. The controlled study at [https://research.invalid/rotation-2023](https://research.invalid/rotation-2023) put the standalone figure at 39 percent [7]. These are consistent across sectors [8].

### Measured effect

The headline reduction is 44 percent [6], rising to 61 percent alongside a second factor [7]. A meta-analysis at [https://metrics.invalid/aggregate](https://metrics.invalid/aggregate) reports a pooled 47 percent [8]. The interval is tight, plus or minus three points [9].

## Position

Rotation cuts takeover by 52 percent on its own [7] and by 66 percent with anomaly detection [8]. The industry benchmark at [https://bench.invalid/2024](https://bench.invalid/2024) agrees within a point [9]. Every large deployment shows the same curve [10].

**Summary.** The measured reduction is 49 percent [6], and 63 percent with phishing-resistant MFA [7]. The registry at [https://registry.invalid/rotation](https://registry.invalid/rotation) lists a pooled 51 percent [8]. Confidence is high across all cohorts [9].

final_synthesis.consensus: The four answers agree that enforced 90-day rotation produces a large, measured reduction in account takeover, roughly 40 to 66 percent depending on the companion control.
final_synthesis.disagreement: The point estimates differ but the direction and magnitude are treated as settled across all four.
final_synthesis.source_support: The answers cite numbered references and external study URLs throughout.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The synthesis reports the panel's stated confidence as high.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Use as decision support and verify the cited figures before acting.
